messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator radius server-group command do?

The aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator radius server-group command configures the switch to use an existing RADIUS server group for 802.1X authentication globally or for a particular port. The no form of the command resets the server group to the default, radius. When configured on a port, the no form of the command resets the server group on that port to the globally configured group. If no global RADIUS server group is configured, the no form of the command resets the configuration to the default group, radius. NOTE When the RADIUS server group for 802.1X authentication is updated on a port, any existing clients on the port that were authenticated using the previous globally configured group will associate with the new group for the port during the next re-authentication cycle. Syntax: no aaa authentication port-access dot1x authenticator radius server-group.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the aaa authentication port-access mac-auth cached-reauth command do?

The aaa authentication port-access mac-auth cached-reauth command enables cached reauthentication on a port. Cached reauthentication allows MAC reauthentications to succeed when the RADIUS server is unavailable. Users who are already authenticated, retain their currently assigned RADIUS attributes. The no form of the command disables cached reauthentication. Configures the period during which an authenticated client, which has failed to reauthenticate because the RADIUS server is unreachable, remains authenticated. The no form of the command resets the cached reauthentication period to the default, 3600 seconds. Specifies the cached reauthentication period (in seconds). Defa ult: 3600. Range: 1 to 4294967295. <PERIOD> Enables and configures the global password that the switch must use for MAC authentication. Syntax: aaa authentication port-access mac-auth cached-reauth-period <PERIOD>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the aaa authentication port-access mac-auth radius server-group <GROUP-NAME> command do?

The aaa authentication port-access mac-auth radius server-group <GROUP-NAME> command configures the MAC authentication server group globally or for a particular port. The no form of the command resets the authentication server group to the default value, radius. When configured on a port, the no form of the command resets the server group on that port to the globally configured group. If no global RADIUS server group is configured, the no form of the command resets the configuration to the default group, radius. NOTE When the RADIUS server group for MAC authentication is updated on a port, any existing clients on the port that were authenticated using the previous globally configured group will associate with the new group for the port during the next re-authentication cycle. Syntax: no aaa authentication port-access mac-auth radius server-group <GROUP-NAME>.

The aaa authentication port-access mac-auth reauth command enables periodic MAC reauthentication of authenticated clients on the port. The no form of the command disables periodic MAC reauthentication on the port. Configures the period after which MAC authenticated clients must be reauthenticated on the port. You must first enable MAC reauthentication on the port before configuring the MAC reauthentication period. The no form of the command resets the MAC reauthentication period to the default, 3600 seconds. Specifies the MAC reauthentication period (in seconds). Default: 3600. Range: 1 to 4294967295. <PERIOD> Clears the MAC authentication statistics associated with the port and all the authenticator state machines associated to this port. If no interface is specified, the statistics is cleared for all MAC authentication enabled ports. Syntax: aaa authentication port-access mac-auth reauth-period <PERIOD>.

Specifies the interface name. <INTERFACE-NAME> Shows information about violations that have occurred and the count of violations for port access authentication methods at the interfaces. Specifies all interfaces. Specifies the interface name or a comma-separated list of inte rfaces, or a hyphen-separated interface range. Shows information on the number of client-limit-exceeded security violations that have occurred. The output can be filtered by interface. Specifies all interfaces. Public show port-access port-security violation client-li. Syntax: show interface <INTERFACE-NAME>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the qos egress-rewrite-map local-priority <PRIORITY-RANGE> {cos <PCP-VALUE>} command do?

The qos egress-rewrite-map local-priority <PRIORITY-RANGE> {cos <PCP-VALUE>} command configures a system-wide mapping from internal local priority to an 802.1Q VLAN priority code point (PCP) or DSCP value. The qos egress-rewrite cos command allows the PCP value to be rewritten on egress, and the qos egress-rewrite dscp command allows the DSCP value to be rewritten on egress. The local priority associated with a packet affects only its behavior within the switch. By default, it does not influence the priority value written to the packet upon transmission. This command, along with its no form, accepts a single local priority, a range of local priorities, or a comma-separated list of local priorities and ranges. This provides a convenient method for configuring multiple entries in the map. Syntax: no qos egress-rewrite-map local-priority <PRIORITY-RANGE> {cos <PCP-VALUE>}.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the qos queue-profile <NAME> command do?

The qos queue-profile <NAME> command creates a new QoS queue profile and switches to the config-queue context for the profile. Or, if the specified QoS queue profile exists, this command switches to the config-queue context for the profile. A queue profile maps queues to local-priority values. Each profile has one to eight queues numbered 0 to 7. The larger the queue number, the higher its priority during transmission scheduling. The no form of this command removes the specified QoS queue profile. Only profiles that are not currently applied can be removed. Specifies the name of the QoS queue profile to create or config ure. Range: 1 to 64 alphanumeric characters, including period (.), underscore (_), and hyphen (-). <NAME>. Syntax: no qos queue-profile <NAME>.

Shows the status of all queue profiles, or a specific queue profile. <NAME> Specifies the name of a queue profile. Range 1 to 64 alphanum eric characters, including period (.), underscore (_), and hyphen (-). [factory-default] Specifies the factory default queue profile. vsx-peer Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show running-config (radius-proxy) command do?

The show running-config (radius-proxy) command displays the current running configuration. Configures the RADIUS server timeout interval in seconds. The proxy waits for this time interval to get a response from the home server. This value should be equal to or greater than the NAS RADIUS timeout value. The no form of this command resets the RADIUS server timeout interval to its default value of 5 seconds. Public timeout (RADIUS proxy) 4007 RADIUS server timeout interval in seconds. Default: 5 seconds. <SECONDS>. Syntax: show running-config.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the no aaa accounting port-access command do?

The no aaa accounting port-access command configures port access accounting information that is captured for 802.1X and MAC-authenticated clients. Defines port access accounting as being local (with the parameter local) (the default). Or defines port access accounting as being remote (with the parameter group <GROUP-NAME>) with a sequence of remote RADIUS servers in a single RADIUS server group to be accessed for port access accounting purposes. For remote RADIUS port access accounting, the information is sent to the first reachable remote RADIUS server in the specified group. If a user-defined RADIUS server group is named in your command, it must exist. The no form of this command works as follows: • no aaa accounting port-access: Globally unconfigures port access accounting. Syntax:
- no aaa accounting port-access local
- no aaa accounting port-access group
- no aaa accounting port-access interim.

syntax:
- no aaa accounting port-access local
- no aaa accounting port-access group
- no aaa accounting port-access interim

The radius-server status-server interval <10-86400> command configures the time interval in seconds to send the status server requests to the RADIUS server. - radius-server status-server interval 4079.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the radius-server timeout [<1-60>] command do?

The radius-server timeout [<1-60>] command specifies the number of seconds to wait for a response from the RADIUS server before trying the next RADIUS server. If a value is not specified, a default value of 5 seconds is used. You can override this value with a fine-grained per server timeout configured for individual servers. The no form of this command resets the RADIUS global authentication timeout to the default of 5 seconds. Specifies the timeout interval of 1 to 60 seconds. Default: 5 sec onds. Syntax: no radius-server timeout [<1-60>].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the router rip <PROCESS-ID> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command do?

The router rip <PROCESS-ID> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command creates RIP process if not already created and enters the router rip <PROCESS-ID> context for the VRF mentioned. If no VRF is mentioned, a default is used. Only one RIP process is allowed per VRF. The no form of this command deletes the RIP instance for the VRF. If no VRF is mentioned the default is deleted. Specifies name of the RIP process ID. Range: 1-63. <PROCESS-ID> Specifies VRF name. Public RIP v2 Configuration commands 4167. Syntax: no router rip <PROCESS-ID> [vrf <VRF-NAME>].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the clear ip rip statistics command do?

The clear ip rip statistics command clears RIP event statistics. Specifies RIP process ID. Range: 1-63 <PROCESS-ID> all-vrfs Clears statistics for all VRFs. Public RIP v2 clear commands 4184 Selects VRF to clear statistics for. Specifies VRF name. Enables RIP process for RIP enabled IP address configured on interface. The no form of this command disables RIP process on interface. Disables RIP process for RIP enabled IP addresses configured on interface. The no form of this command enables RIP process on interface. Disables an interface from sending RIP packets for a specific IP address. The no form of this command enables interface for sending RIP packets for a specific IP address. Public send disable (rip) 4188 Disables interface from receiving RIP packets for a specific IP address. Syntax: clear ip rip [<PROCESS-ID>] statistics [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>].

Specifies name of the IP prefix lists. <NAME> vsx-peer Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. Syntax: show ip aspath-list [<NAME>] [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show capacities-status rpvst command do?

The show capacities-status rpvst command shows the number of RPVST VLANs and RPVST VPORTs currently configured. Shows the spanning tree mode and information on the RPVST instances. When Port security is enabled on the port and the client is not-yet authenticated, the security feature keeps the port in the Down state. STP also keeps the port in the Blocking state and the role as Disabled in the show spanning-tree command output, whereas in the hardware, the state is maintained as Learning. After client authentication is successful, the port state changes to Forwarding. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. Syntax: show capacities-status rpvst.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show smartlink group command do?

The show smartlink group command shows information for a specific Smartlink group. Specifies Smartlink group ID. <GROUP-ID> Shows information for all configured Smartlink groups. Public show smartlink group all 4422 Shows detailed information for all configured Smartlink groups. Public show smartlink group detail 4423 Shows information for received flush messages. Clears Smartlink statistics for the specified Smartlink group or all Smartlink groups. Specifies Smartlink group. <GROUP-ID> Clears Smartlink flush statistics. Shows current running configuration. Syntax: show smartlink group <GROUP-ID>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show capacities smartlink command do?

The show capacities smartlink command shows Smartlink capacities or Smartlink capacities and status. Enables the notification of events to be sent as traps to the SNMP management stations. It is enabled by default. The no form of this command disables the event traps. Enables sending SNMP traps for LLDP related events from a particular interface. LLDP trap generation is enabled by default on all the interfaces and has to be disabled for interfaces on which traps are not required to be generated. The no form of this command disables the LLDP trap generation. NOTE LLDP trap generation is disabled by default at the global level and must be enabled before any LLDP traps are sent. Syntax: show capacities smartlink | show capacities-status smartlink.

syntax: show capacities smartlink | show capacities-status smartlink

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show rmon alarm command do?

The show rmon alarm command displays the RMON alarm configurations. Specifies the RMON alarm index. Range: 1 to 20. Displays SNMP agent UDP port number. Displays a list of all configured SNMP v1/v2c communities. Displays all configured SNMP contexts. Displays the configured SNMP v3 snmp engine-id. If the SNMP v3 engine-id is not configured, by default a unique engine-id is created by the switch using a combination of the enterprise OID value and the switch's MAC address. Displays the configured SNMP v3 security level. Displays SNMP description, location, and contact information. Displays all configured SNMP traps/informs receivers. Displays all configured SNMP v3 users. For more details on the user enabled status, see snmpv3 security-level. Displays the list of all the configured SNMP views. Syntax: show rmon alarm [index <INDEX>].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the snmp-server community <STRING> [view <VIEW-NAME>] command do?

The snmp-server community <STRING> [view <VIEW-NAME>] command associates an SNMP MIB view with the SNMP community. The no form of this command removes the associated SNMP MIB view from the SNMP community. Specifies the SNMP v1/SNMP v2c community string. Range: 1 to 32 printable ASCII characters, excluding space and question ma rk. Specifies the view name for the SNMP MIB view. Accepts a maxi mum of 32 characters. <STRING> <VIEW-NAME>. Syntax: no snmp-server community <STRING> [view <VIEW-NAME>].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the snmp-server community <STRING> command do?

The snmp-server community <STRING> command adds an SNMP v1/SNMP v2c community string. A community string is like a password that controls read/ write access to the SNMP agent. A network management program must supply this name when attempting to get SNMP information from the switch. A maximum of 10 community strings are supported. Once you create your own community string, the default community string (public) is deleted. The no form of this command removes the specified SNMP v1/SNMP v2c community string. When no community string exists, a default community string with the value public is automatically defined. <STRING> Specifies the SNMP v1/SNMP v2c community string. Range: 1 to 32 printable ASCII characters, excluding space and question ma rk. Syntax: no snmp-server community <STRING>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the snmp-server snmpv3-only command do?

The snmp-server snmpv3-only command accepts SNMP v3 messages only, SNMP v1 and SNMP v2c will be disabled. By default SNMP v1, SNMP v2c and SNMP v3 will all be enabled. The no form of this command restores the default setting and reenables SNMP v1 and SNMP v2c. Syntax:
- no snmp-server snmpv3-only
- snmp-server snmpv3-only 4490.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the snmp-server trap aaa-server-reachability-status command do?

The snmp-server trap aaa-server-reachability-status command enables the SNMP trap for AAA server status. When enabled, traps are sent whenever AAA server (RADIUS, TACACS) status changes from reachable to unreachable and vice versa. The no form of this command disables sending SNMP trap for AAA server status. Syntax:
- no snmp-server trap aaa-server-reachability-status
- snmp-server trap aaa-server-reachability-status 4491.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ssh host-key-algorithms command do?

The ssh host-key-algorithms command configures SSH to use a set of host key algorithms in the specified priority order. Host key algorithms specify which host key types are allowed to be used for the SSH connection. The first host key entered in the CLI is considered a first priority. Each option represents a type of key that can be used. Host keys are used to verify the host that you are connecting to. This configuration allows you to control which host key types are presented to incoming clients, or which host key types to receive first from hosts. Only the host key algorithms that are specified by the user are configured. The no form of this command removes the configuration of host key algorithms and reverts SSH to use the default set of algorithms. Syntax: ssh host-key-algorithms <HOST-KEY-ALGORITHMS-LIST>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show environment power-consumption command do?

The show environment power-consumption command displays power consumption information. Displays detailed power consumption information. For VSF supported platforms only. Displays the power consum ption information for the specified VSF member. Range: 1-10. Power consumed values are updated every 5 seconds. The total power consumed is the total power used in a chassis. The power consumed average is calculated from the total power consumed as a running average over a period of time. The average period has a default of 10 minutes. The period can be configured using power-consumption-average-period. On chassis where power input is not supported, power consumed is displayed as Power Usage and PSU Output Power is displayed as N/A. Syntax: show environment power-consumption [detail].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ssh certificate-as-authorized-key command do?

The ssh certificate-as-authorized-key command enables SSH enforcement that the username must be present in the certificate that is being used for authorization. This configuration alters how certificate-based authentication maps to a user account. When this is enabled, SSH will not require local user association with an authorized-key and instead enforces that the username used to log in is present within the certificate. The SSH server will check for the username in certificate fields Common Name or User Principle Name for a match. If a certificate is not used for authentication then this configuration has no effect on SSH authentication. The no form of this command disables the SSH enforcement of username in the certificate. Syntax: no ssh certificate-as-authorized-key.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show user-group command do?

The show user-group command shows user group information for the built-in groups plus any user-defined local user groups. When entered without <GROUP-NAME>, summary information is shown for all groups. Narrows the show command output to that of the specified gro up, and for local user groups, adds the User Group Rules list. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. <GROUP-NAME> vsx-peer Shows the following information for the logged-in user: • User name. • User authentication type: local, RADIUS, or TACACS+. • User group: administrators, operators, or <GROUP-NAME>. Syntax: show user-group [<GROUP-NAME>] [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show ubt information command do?

The show ubt information command shows SAC and UAC information for UBT. Specifying a zone name displays UBT information for that zone. Specifies UBT zone name. Maximum characters: 64. ZONE-NAME. Syntax: show ubt information zone <ZONE-NAME>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show ubt state zone <ZONE-NAME> command do?

The show ubt state zone <ZONE-NAME> command shows the global UBT state. Specifying a zone shows the UBT state of that zone. Specifying a UAC IP address shows the UBT state of that UAC. zone <ZONE-NAME> Specifies UBT zone name. Maximum characters: 64. uac-ip <UAC-ADDR> Specifies the IP address of the user anchor controller for which to view user information. Specify the address in IPv4 format (x. x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. Syntax: show ubt state zone <ZONE-NAME> uac-ip <UAC-ADDR>.

The show vlan port command displays the VLANs configured for a specific layer 2 interface. <INTERFACE-ID> vsx-peer Specifies an interface ID. Format: member/slot/port. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. Displays a summary of the VLAN configuration on the switch. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the vlan <VLAN-LIST> command do?

The vlan <VLAN-LIST> command creates a VLAN and changes to the config-vlan-id context for the VLAN. By default, the VLAN is enabled. To disable a VLAN, use the shutdown command. If the specified VLAN exists, this command changes to the config-vlan-id context for the VLAN. If a range of VLANs is specified, the context does not change. NOTE VLANs used for internal purposes using the command system internal vlan range cannot be used for any other (L2) purposes. The no form of this command removes a VLAN. VLAN 1 is the default VLAN and cannot be deleted. Specifies a single ID, or a series of IDs separated by commas (2, 3, 4), dashes (2-4), or both (2-4, 6). Range: 1 to 4094. <VLAN-LIST>. Syntax: no vlan <VLAN-LIST>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the vlan access command do?

The vlan access command creates an access interface and assigns an VLAN ID to it. Only one VLAN ID can be assigned to each access interface. VLANs can only be assigned to non-routed (Layer 2) interfaces. All interfaces are non-routed (Layer 2) by default when created. Use routing and no routing commands to move ports between Layer 3 and Layer 2 interfaces. The no form of this command removes an access VLAN from the interface in the current context and sets it to the default VLAN ID of 1. Specifies a single ID, or a series of IDs separated by commas (2, 3, 4), dashes (2-4), or both (2-4, 6). Range: 1 to 4094. <VLAN-ID>. Syntax: vlan access <VLAN-ID>.

The vlan protocol <PROTOCOL_NAME> command adds protocol mapping to a VLAN on an interface. The no form of this command removes protocol mapping from the VLAN on an interface. Public vlan protocol 5041 Specifies a VLAN ID. Range: 2 to 4094. <VLAN-ID> <PROTOCOL_NAME>. Syntax: no vlan protocol <PROTOCOL_NAME>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show active-gateway <IFNAME> command do?

The show active-gateway <IFNAME> command displays the gateway information per SVI, such as: • Active-Gateway IPv4 and its MAC address • Active-Gateway IPv6 and its MAC address Specifies the VSX interface name. <IFNAME> vsx-peer Displays a virtual IPv4/IPv6 and MAC configured for active-active routing. Specifies the VLAN name. Syntax: string <VLAN-NAME> vsx-peer Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. Public show interface <VLAN-NAME> 5143 Displays a specified LAG or all configured LAGs along with VSX LAGs. Specifies the LAG name. Optional. Syntax: string <LAG-NAME> vsx-peer Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. Syntax: show active-gateway <IFNAME>.

The vsx active-forwarding command configures VSX active-forwarding on an interface VLAN. The no form of this command unconfigures VSX active-forwarding on a VLAN interface. Syntax: no vsx active-forwarding.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the vsx shutdown-on-split command do?

The vsx shutdown-on-split command shuts down the configured non-VSX interfaces on the VSX secondary along with VSX interfaces during a VSX split. The no form of this command resumes the non-VSX interfaces that are shut down during the VSX split. NOTE This command has no effect on the VSX primary during a split. However, when applied on the VSX primary, the command will bring down the non-VSX interfaces until linkup delay timer expires during the VSX primary reboot. Public vsx shutdown-on-split 5193. Syntax: no vsx shutdown-on-split.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the vsx-sync {[active-gateways] [policies]} command do?

The vsx-sync {[active-gateways] [policies]} command enables VSX sync of active gateways or policies associated under an interface. To synchronize the associations, you must configure the same interface vlan on the peer switch. The no form of this command removes VSX synchronization for active gateways or policies associated under an interface, but it does not remove the feature configurations from the secondary peer switch. {[active-gateways] [policies]} access-gateways policies. Syntax: no vsx-sync {[active-gateways] [policies]}.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the vsx-sync aaa command do?

The vsx-sync aaa command enables VSX synchronization of all AAA configurations, including user, RADIUS server, and TACACS+ server, on the primary VSX node to the secondary peer switch. The no form of this command removes VSX synchronization of global AAA configurations, but it does not remove the existing global AAA feature configurations from the secondary peer switch. Syntax: no vsx-sync aaa.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the vsx-sync acl-log-timer command do?

The vsx-sync acl-log-timer command enables VSX synchronization of access list log timer configurations on the primary VSX node to the secondary peer. The no form of this command removes VSX synchronization of access list log timer configurations to the secondary peer. However, it does not remove the previously synced configurations from the secondary peer switch. Syntax: no vsx-sync acl-log-timer.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the vsx-sync arp-security command do?

The vsx-sync arp-security command enables VSX synchronization of the ARP security configurations on the primary VSX switch to the secondary peer switch. After you enter vsx-sync arp-security, you must enter vsx-sync mclag-int erfaces for enabling VSX synchronization for the ARP security feature. The no form of this command removes VSX synchronization of ARP security configurations on VLAN mode and LAG interface mode to the secondary peer switch. However, it does not remove the existing ARP security configurations from the secondary peer switch. Syntax: no vsx-sync arp-security.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the vsx-sync bfd-global command do?

The vsx-sync bfd-global command enables syncing of global BFD configurations, such as echo-src-ip-address, detect-multip lier, min-transmit-interval, and min-receive-interval, on the primary VSX node to the secondary peer. NOTE This command enables VSX synchronization only at the top level and not at the context level. The no form of this command disables the syncing of global BFD configurations to the secondary peer, but it does not remove the existing global BFD feature configurations from it. Syntax:
- no vsx-sync bfd-global
- vsx-sync bfd-global 5210.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the vsx-sync bgp command do?

The vsx-sync bgp command enables syncing of BGP configurations on the primary VSX switch to the secondary peer switch. The no form of this command disables syncing BGP, as path lists, community lists, prefix lists, and route map configurations to the secondary peer, but it does not remove the previously synced configurations from the secondary peer switch. Syntax: no vsx-sync bgp.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the vsx-sync dhcp-relay command do?

The vsx-sync dhcp-relay command enables VSX synchronization of DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 relay configurations on the primary VSX node to the secondary peer. The no form of the command disables the VSX synchronization of DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 relay configurations to the secondary peer; however, it does not remove the existing DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 relay configurations from the secondary VSX peer. Syntax: no vsx-sync dhcp-relay.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the vsx-sync dhcp-server command do?

The vsx-sync dhcp-server command enables VSX synchronization of all DHCPv4 server configurations, including external storage configurations, on the primary VSX node to the secondary peer. Only the primary VSX node answers DHCP service requests, and leases can only be exported from the primary VSX node. The no form of the command disables VSX synchronization of DHCPv4 server configurations to the secondary peer; however, it does not remove the existing DHCPv4 server feature configurations from the secondary peer. Syntax: no vsx-sync dhcp-server.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the vsx-sync dhcp-snooping command do?

The vsx-sync dhcp-snooping command enables VSX synchronization of DHCP snooping configurations on the primary node to the secondary peer switch. To synchronize DHCP snooping configurations associated with a particular VLAN and interface, configure the same VLAN and interface on the peer device. The no form of this command disables syncing DHCP snooping configurations to the secondary peer, but it does not remove the previously synced configurations from the secondary peer. Syntax:
- no vsx-sync dhcp-snooping
- vsx-sync dhcp-snooping 5217.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the vsx-sync keychain command do?

The vsx-sync keychain command enables synchronizing of key chain configurations on primary VSX node to the secondary peer. There is no configuration synchronization from secondary to primary peer. If any additional modification or configuration is made on the primary for the key chain set of features, the features will be auto-synchronized. The no form of the command disables synchronizing key chain configurations to the secondary peer. But it does not remove the previously synchronized configurations from the secondary peer. Syntax: no vsx-sync keychain.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the vsx-sync mclag-interfaces command do?

The vsx-sync mclag-interfaces command enables the VSX synchronization of VSX LAG interface associations and attributes on the primary VSX switch to the secondary peer switch. The Usage section in this topic provides a listing of specific associations and attributes that are synchronized to the secondary switch. The no form of this command removes VSX synchronization of global VSX LAG and attributes, but it does not remove the existing VSX LAG feature configurations from the secondary peer switch. Syntax: no vsx-sync mclag-interfaces.

The no form of this command disables syncing ND snooping configurations to the secondary peer, but it does not remove the previously synced configurations from the secondary peer. messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the vsx-sync neighbor command do?

The vsx-sync neighbor command enables VSX synchronization of IPv4 and IPv6 static neighbors configuration on primary VSX node to the secondary peer. There is no configuration sync from secondary to primary peer. If any new modification or additional configuration is made on the primary node for IPv4 and IPv6 static neighbors configuration, they will be auto-synced. The no form of this command VSX synchronization of IPv4 and IPv6 static neighbors configurations to the secondary peer, but it does not remove the previously synced configurations from the secondary peer switch. Syntax: no vsx-sync neighbor.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the vsx-sync ospf command do?

The vsx-sync ospf command enables syncing of OSPF (including OSPFv2 and OPSFv3), route map, and key chain configurations on the primary VSX switch. There is no configuration sync from secondary to primary peer. To synchronize OSPF configurations at the port level context, configure the same port on the peer device. Public vsx-sync ospf 5233 The no form of this command disables syncing of OSPF, route map, and key chain configurations to the secondary peer. But it does not remove the previously synced configurations from the secondary peer switch. NOTE The OSPF router ID is not synchronized. This exclusion is needed because the router ID uniquely identifies the router. The two OSPF routers with the same router ID do not form an adjacency between them. Syntax: no vsx-sync ospf.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the vsx-sync private-vlan-global command do?

The vsx-sync private-vlan-global command enable sync for private VLAN global configuration on the primary VSX node to the secondary peer. Private VLAN global configuration syncing is supported on the switch by configuring the feature name under vsx configuration context. There are no parameters in this command. The no form of this command disables Private VLAN global configuration syncing to the secondary peer. Syntax: no vsx-sync private-vlan-global.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the vsx-sync qos-global command do?

The vsx-sync qos-global command enables the VSX synchronization of global QoS configurations, such as CoS map, DSCP map, and trust policy, on the primary VSX node to the secondary peer switch. To enable VSX synchronization at the context level for this feature, enter the vsx-sync qos command at the context level. The no form of this command removes VSX synchronization of global QoS configurations, but it does not remove the existing global QoS feature configurations from the secondary peer switch. Syntax: no vsx-sync qos-global.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the vsx-sync route-map command do?

The vsx-sync route-map command enables syncing of all As Path lists, community lists, prefix lists, and route map configurations on primary VSX node to the secondary peer switch. There is no configuration sync from the secondary to primary peer. Public vsx-sync route-map 5240 The no form of this command disables syncing of As Path lists, community lists, prefix lists, and route map configurations to the secondary peer, but it does not remove the previously synced configurations from the secondary peer switch. NOTE When vsx-sync for BGP or OSPF is configured, route map configurations are synchronized from primary VSX node to the secondary peer due to the dependency in configurations. Syntax: no vsx-sync route-map.

The no form of this command removes VSX synchronization of global STP configurations, but it does not remove the existing global STP feature configurations from the secondary peer switch. messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the vsx-sync vrrp command do?

The vsx-sync vrrp command enables VSX synchronization of all VRRP configurations on the primary VSX node to the secondary peer switch. There is no configuration sync from secondary to primary peer. To synchronize VRRP configurations at the port level context, the same port must be configured on the peer device with IP address. Public vsx-sync vrrp 5252 The no form of this command disables syncing VRRP configurations to the secondary peer, but it does not remove the previously synced configurations from the secondary peer. NOTE BFD IP is the IP address of VRRP peer device. Hence it cannot be synced. In the owner scenario, in case the priority is synced, both VSX primary and secondary devices will have 255 as their priority. Syntax: no vsx-sync vrrp.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show interface vxlan vni command do?

The show interface vxlan vni command shows detailed VNI information for the VXLAN interface. Specifies the number for a given VNI. Range: 1 to 16777214. Shows the VTEPS information for a specified VNI. transport-VRFshows the transport VRFstatus for a specified VNI. Displays detailed VTEP information for the VXLAN interface. VTEP states can be one of the following: • operational: Tunnel endpoint is fully programmed on the switch hardware. • configuration_error: Tunnel endpoint programming in the switch hardware failed due to misconfiguration. • no_hw_resources: Tunnel endpoint programming failed in the switch hardware due to insufficient resources. • activating: Initial state of tunnel endpoint when it is configured. When a tunnel endpoint is a directly connected via nexthop, then nexthop reachability appears empty (--). Syntax: show interface vxlan vni <VNI-ID> [vteps | transport-vrf].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the erase all zeroize command do?

The erase all zeroize command restores the switch to its factory default configuration. You will be prompted before the procedure starts. Once complete, the switch will restart from the primary image with factory default settings. Syntax: erase [all] zeroize.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the diag event-trap-disable command do?

The diag event-trap-disable command prevents the specific event notifications from being sent as traps to the SNMP management stations. The no form of this command disables this feature and reenables the notification of events to be sent as traps. Specify up to five comma separated Event IDs To modify the traps to be disabled, such as changing the existing list of event IDs or removing an event ID from the blocked trap list, first completely remove previously configured disabled traps using the CLI command no diag event-trap-disable <EVENT-ID>. After that, reconfigure the new event IDs or set of event IDs for the changes to take effect. The command to disable traps cannot be issued via network management systems such as SNMP, the REST API, HPE Aruba Networking Fabric Composer or HPE Aruba Networking Central. Syntax: diag event-trap-disable <EVENT-IDS>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the guide say about IPv6 Example?

IPv6 Example: switch(config)# logrotate target tftp://2001: db8: 0: 1: : 128 Public Resetting the time frequency to daily 33 I d e n t i f y i n g a r e m o t e h o s t f o r r e c e i v i n g r o t a t e d l o... Remote transfer of rotated log files Only the TFTP protocol is supported for remote transfer, and both IPv4 and IPv6 addresses are supported. Only newly rotated log files are transferred to the remote host during the log rotation. Previously rotated log files are not transferred. After a file is successfully transferred, it is removed from the switch local path. Packet level failures with TFTP are handled in the protocol itself. With each TFTP session failure, TFTP retries the file transfer three times. Retries have a timeout of five seconds.

document_title: Diagnostics And Supportability Guide (6300, 6400 Switch Series)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the logrotate period {daily | hourly | monthly | weekly} command do?

The logrotate period {daily | hourly | monthly | weekly} command sets the log file rotation time period. Defaults to daily. A log file that exceeds either the logrotate maxsize or the logrotate period (whichever happens first), triggers rotation of the log file. The no form of this command resets the log rotation period to the default of daily. Rotates log files on a daily basis (default) at 0: 01. Rotates log files every hour at the first second of the hour. Rotates log files monthly on the first day of the month at 00: 01. Rotates log files once a week on Sunday at 00: 01. Setting a weekly period: switch(config)# logrotate period weekly Resetting the period to its default of daily: switch(config)# no logrotate period. Syntax: logrotate period {daily | hourly | monthly | weekly}.

The copy checkpoint command copies the checkpoint using TFTP, SFTP, SCP, or USB. messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, how do you troubleshoot?

To troubleshoot, follow the documented procedure: 1. Modify the ACL to allow the ping traffic 2. Unapply the ACL from egress (8400/8320/8325/9300/9300S switches) or Control Plane 3. Ping a destination which is not matched by the ACL. For example, if the ACL is blocking traffic based. Commands shown in the procedure include: PING 100.1.2.10 (100.1.2.10) 100(128) bytes of data; ping: sendmsg: Operation not permitted; ping: sendmsg: Operation not permitted; ping: sendmsg: Operation not permitted; ping: sendmsg: Operation not permitted; ping: sendmsg: Operation not permitted.

The cat <FILENAME/DIRECTORY-NAME> command prints the contents of a file to the console. messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the cp [options] <SOURCE-FILENAME/SOURCE-DIRECTORY> command do?

The cp [options] <SOURCE-FILENAME/SOURCE-DIRECTORY> command copies files or directories. Selects the options for the command. Specifies the preservation of symlinks (default if -R). Same as -dpR. Specifies recursiveness, all files, and subdirectories are copied. Specifies the following of all symlinks. Specifies the following of symlinks on command line. Specifies the preservation of file attributes if possible. Public cp 179 Specifies the overwriting of a file or directory. Specifies the prompting before an overwrite. Specifies the creation of (sym) links. Specifies the name of the source file or directory. Specifies the name of the destination file or directory. Syntax: cp [options] <SOURCE-FILENAME/SOURCE-DIRECTORY>.

Specify address, netmask, and gateway as A. B. C. D. Syntax: ip {show | dhcp | disable | addr <ADDR-NETMASK-GATEWAY>}.

Shows entries which start with a period (.). Shows output similar to -a, but excludes a period (.) and a dou ble period (..). Shows output list by columns. Shows output list by lines. Shows listing of directory entries instead of contents Follows symlinks. Follows symlinks on the command line. Recurse. Appends a slash (/) to directory entries. Appends an indicator to entries. An indicator can be as an aster isk (*) or slash (/) or equal sign (=) or at sign (@) or pipe (|). Shows the output in a long listing format. Shows the list inode numbers. Shows a list of numeric UIDs and GIDs instead of names. Syntax: ls [<OPTIONS>] [<FILE-NME>].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the system serviceos password-prompt command do?

The system serviceos password-prompt command use this command to enable password authentication for ServiceOS. By default, the ServiceOS shell (accessible only from the local switch console port) requires no password to login as an admin use. When this setting is enabled, the same password used to authenticate the admin user in the AOS-CX CLI or WebUI can be used to log in to the ServiceOS shell. If this setting is enabled, a forgotten admin user password cannot be reset using ServiceOS; if there are no other local or RADIUS/TACACS user accounts with administrator-level access, the switch must be zeroized by entering the username zeroize command at the ServiceOS login prompt to restore administrator access. serviceos password-prompt cannot be enabled if the built-in admin user has been disabled. Syntax: system serviceos password-prompt.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the tftp {-b | -g | -l <LOCAL-FILE> | -p | -r <REMOTE-FILE>} host [<PORT>] command do?

The tftp {-b | -g | - - <LOCAL-FILE> | -p | -r <REMOTE-FILE>} host [<PORT>] command transfers files to and from a remote machine (TFTP a file). Public tftp 207 {-b | -g | -l | -p | -r <REMOTE-FILE>} Selects the options for transferring a file. -b -g -l -p Specifies the transfer blocks of size octets. The default blocksi ze is set to 1468, which can be overridden with the -b option. Specifies to get a file. Specifies a local file. Specifies to put a file in remote location. -r <REMOTE-FILE> Specifies a remote file. Specifies the port for transfer. If no port option is specified, TFT P uses the standard UDP port 69 by default. <PORT>. Syntax: tftp {-b | -g | - - <LOCAL-FILE> | -p | -r <REMOTE-FILE>} host [<PORT>].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show troubleshoot command do?

The show troubleshoot command displays the history of past troubleshooting sessions. Include the history parameter to display an overview of all troubleshooting commands executed on the switch within the past 30 minutes, including the instance IDs of each troubleshooting session, the name of the feature, and timestamps for the start and end of each session. Use the details parameter to display details for an individual session. details instance <1-1000> history. Syntax: show troubleshoot {details instance <1-10000>}|history.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the troubleshoot classifier command do?

The troubleshoot classifier command troubleshoot classifier features, including ACLs, counters, policies, policers, range-checkers and TCAM values. Troubleshoot Access Control Lists (ACLs). counter Troubleshoot TCAM counter allocation. Troubleshoot classifier feature health. Issue the command trou bleshoot classifier health to display health information for all c lassifier features, or include the health parameter after a featu re name (for example, troubleshoot classifier acl health) to di splay health information for that feature only. Include the optio nal verbose parameter to display detailed troubleshooting prog ress. Troubleshoot issues with policer resource allocation. Troubleshoot issues with classifier policies. Troubleshoot issues with range-checker resources. Troubleshoot issues with TCAM resource allocation. Syntax: troubleshoot classifier.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the troubleshoot client-insight clientmac command do?

The troubleshoot client-insight clientmac command troubleshoot any client configuration or operational issues found via Client Insight. Public troubleshoot client-insight 241 health [verbose] operations parameters "<mac-addr>" Display health information for client-insight errors on the netw ork, including the error message, message ID, severity level, an d when the message was last reported. Include the optional ver bose parameter to display all available troubleshooting details f rom client insight log files. Display comprehensive client insight feature diagnostics with a detailed analysis. Display detailed diagnostics by including the MAC address of th e client to troubleshoot. Note that the MAC address must be en closed in double quotation marks ("). Syntax: troubleshoot client-insight clientmac.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the troubleshoot l3 command do?

The troubleshoot l3 command troubleshoot issues with Layer-3 features and protocols. Troubleshoot the Address Resolution Protocol (ARP). By defaul t, the troubleshoot l3 arp command displays both feature heal th and feature operations troubleshooting information. Include t he optional health or operations parameters to display inform ation for that category only. Issue the troubleshoot l3 Config command to display the resul ts of configuration validation checks for all L3 features. Include the config parameter after a specific L3 component to display c onfiguration validation information for that component only. By default, the troubleshoot l3 dhcpv-relay command displays feature configuration, health, and operations troubleshooting in formation. Syntax: troubleshoot l3.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the troubleshoot multicast command do?

The troubleshoot multicast command use the troubleshoot multicast commands to troubleshoot multicast networks while focusing on specific multicast settings. This targeted approach to troubleshooting helps identify and fix issues in large, complex networks that include multiple virtual routing domains (VRFs) and interfaces. AOS-CX troubleshooting works with both IPv4 and IPv6 multicast protocols, and can address multicast group membership problems with IGMP and MLD, and verify the routing paths for multicast traffic using PIM or PIM6. Syntax: troubleshoot multicast.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the troubleshoot flow-telemetry command do?

The troubleshoot flow-telemetry command perform troubleshooting on flow telemetry issues for basic health, incorrect configurations, application performance, application recognition failures, and ipfix flow exporters and flow records. When you issue the troubleshoot flow-telemetry command for switches that support both application recognition and IPFIX, the output of the command displays information for both features. app-recognition [config| [health [verbose]}] Troubleshoot the application recognition feature. By default, the troubleshoot flow-telemetry app-recognition command displays information for the feature configuration an d health. Include the config or health parameters to display in formation of that type only. Syntax: troubleshoot flow-telemetry.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, how do you configure IRDP?

To configure IRDP, follow the documented procedure: 1. Enable IRDP on an interface with the command ip irdp 2. Set the maximum hold time with the command ip irdp holdtime 3. Set the maximum router advertisement interval with the command ip irdp maxadvertinterv.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip irdp command do?

The ip irdp command enables IRDP on an interface and specifies the packet type that is used to send advertisements. By default, the packet type is set to multicast. IRDP is only supported on layer 3 interfaces. The no form of this command disables IRDP on an interface. Public ip irdp 23 Advertisements are sent as broadcast packets to IP address 25 5.255.255.255. Advertisements are sent as multicast packets to the multicast g roup with IP address 24.0.0.1. Default. On the HPE Aruba Networking 6400 Switch Series, interface identification differs. Enabling IRDP on interface 1/1/1 with packet type set to the default value (multicast). switch(config)# interface 1/1/1 switch(config-if)# ip irdp Enabling IRDP on interface 1/1/1 with packet type set to broadcast. Syntax: ip irdp [broadcast | multicast].

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what is the syntax of the ip irdp command?
The syntax of the ip irdp command is: ip irdp [broadcast | multicast]

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip irdp holdtime command do?

The ip irdp holdtime command specifies the maximum amount of time the host will consider an advertisement to be valid until a newer advertisement arrives. When a new advertisement arrives, hold time is reset. Hold time must be greater than or equal to the maximum advertisement interval. Therefore, if the hold time for an advertisement expires, the host can reasonably conclude that the router interface that sent the advertisement is no longer available. The default hold time is three times the maximum advertisement interval. The no form of this command removes the specified maximum amount of time the host will consider an advertisement to be valid until a newer advertisement arrives and update it to the default value. Specifies the lifetime of router advertisements sent from this in terface. Range: 4 to 9000 seconds. Syntax: ip irdp holdtime <TIME>.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

The syntax of the ip irdp holdtime command is: ip irdp holdtime <TIME>

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip irdp maxadvertinterval command do?

The ip irdp maxadvertinterval command specifies the maximum router advertisement interval. The no form of this command removes the specified maximum router advertisement interval and reverts to the default value. Specifies the maximum time allowed between the sending of u nsolicited router advertisements. Range: 4 to 1800 seconds. De fault: 600 seconds. Public ip irdp maxadvertinterval 26. Syntax: ip irdp maxadvertinterval <TIME>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip igmp snooping (config mode) command do?

The ip igmp snooping (config mode) command configures drop-unknown and fastlearn modes on the ports. While IGMP snooping is enabled, the traffic will be forwarded only to ports that made an IGMP request for the multicast. Drop unknown filters ensure that packets are not forwarded to ports that did not make a request for the traffic stream. This could either be a filter across all VLANs (vlan-shared) or per VLAN (vlan-exclusive). The default is vlan-shared. Fast learn enables the port to learn group information when receiving a topology change notification. By default, fast learn is not enabled on ports. Public IGMP snooping commands 36 Drop unknown filters ensure that packets are not forwarded to ports that did not make a request for the traffic stream. Enables a shared VLAN filter on the switch. Default is vlan-sh ared. Syntax: ip igmp snooping.

The ip igmp snooping (vlan mode) command these commands enable or disable IP IGMP snooping on the VLAN, create IGMP snooping static multicast groups, set the IGMP snooping version and Configure the ACL on a particular interface. NOTE Disabling and enabling IGMP snooping on a VLAN causes IGMP querier re- election. | Parameter | | Description | | | | access-list | | Associates an ACL with the IGMP. | | | Enables or disables IGMP snooping on the VLAN. Starting with enable|disable AOS-CX 10.16, IGMP snooping is enabled by default. | no... | | Negates any configured parameter. | | | | | Public | | ip igmp snooping (vlan mode) | 41 | static-group <MULTICAST-IP- ADDRESS> This parameter configures an IGMP snooping static multicast g roup. Specify the IGMP static multicast group IP address in A. B. C. D format. Syntax: ip igmp snooping.

The no form of this command removes the configuration. Specifies the access list name. Public ip igmp snooping apply access list 44. Syntax: ip igmp snooping apply access list <ACL-NAME>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the guide say about Interface counters polling?

Interface counters polling: • Agent IP configuration for IPv4 and IPv6 • Header size configuration • Max datagram size configuration • Ingress sampling for all forwarded traffic (L2, L3) • Egress sampling for all forwarded traffic (L2, L3) • Enable/Disable sFlow per interface • Support for three remote collectors • A collector can be defined on the default and non-default VRF • Sampling on Ethernet and LAG interfaces • High availability support (sampling continues to work after switch-over) • Source IP support (setting source IP for sFlow datagrams sent to a remote collector) Limitations • Sampling rate cannot be set per interface (global only) • sFlow is not configurable through SNMP.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the clear sflow statistics command do?

The clear sflow statistics command this command clears the sFlow sample statistics counter to 0 either globally or for a specific interface. Specifies all interfaces on the switch. interface <INTERFACE-NAME> Specifies the name of an interface on the switch. Syntax: clear sflow statistics {global | interface <INTERFACE-NAME>}.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the sflow agent-ip <IP-ADDR> command do?

The sflow agent-ip <IP-ADDR> command defines the IP address of the sFlow agent to use in sFlow datagrams. This address must be defined for sFlow to function. HPE recommends that the address: • can uniquely identify the switch • is reachable by the sFlow collector • does not change with time The no form of this command deletes the IP address of the sFlow agent. This causes sFlow to stop working and no datagrams will be sent to the sFlow collector. <IP-ADDR> Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255, or IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: x xxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number f rom 0 to F. The agent address is used to identify the switch in a ll sFlow datagrams sent to sFlow collectors. Syntax: sflow agent-ip <IP-ADDR>.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what is the syntax of the sflow agent-ip <IP-ADDR> command?
The syntax of the sflow agent-ip <IP-ADDR> command is: sflow agent-ip <IP-ADDR>

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the sflow mode {ingress | egress | both} command do?

The sflow mode {ingress | egress | both} command sets the sFlow sampling mode. The default mode is ingress. The no form of the command sets the sampling mode to ingress. Executing the no form of the command with the ingress option will have no impact as ingress is the default mode. | Parameter | | Description | | | | ingress | | Samples only ingress traffic. | | | | egress | | Samples only egress traffic. | | | | both | | Samples both ingress and egress traffic. Syntax: sflow mode {ingress | egress | both}.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the IPv6 dhcp command do?

The IPv6 dhcp command configure to enable DHCPv6 mode on a VLAN. In DHCPv6 Mode, the VLAN will get a DHCP-Server allocated IPv6 address along with DNS and NTP attributes. The no form of the command disables DHCP mode and is supported only on interface VLANs; it is not supported on the management interface. NOTE ZTPv6 attributes are included in the request packet, but they are not utilized by ZTP on the data port. Currently, ZTPv6 attributes are only supported on the management port. Also, IPv6 addresses assigned through DHCPv6 only support a /64 prefix. Syntax: IPv6 dhcp.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip dhcp option command do?

The ip dhcp option command this command enables the DHCP client host name and broadcast flag globally. If the ip dhcp option broadcast-flag command is enabled, then the DHCP offer and ack packets in the DHCP requests will be treated as broadcast packets. These packets will not be forwarded due to the presence of a default static route. The no form of this command globally disables the host name and DHCP client broadcast flag options. NOTE The ip dhcp option broadcast-flag command should be configured before configuring the ip dhcp command. Syntax: ip dhcp option [host-name | broadcast-flag].

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

The syntax of the ip dhcp option command is: ip dhcp option [host-name | broadcast-flag]

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the no ip dhcp preferred-vlan vlan <VLAN-ID> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command do?

The no ip dhcp preferred-vlan vlan <VLAN-ID> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command specifies the preferred VLAN for DHCP client options, including DNS, NTP, and Default Gateway. The preferred VLAN is the VLAN used to select DHCP client options within a specified VRF when multiple VLANs have DHCP client enabled in the same VRF. If no VRF is specified, the configuration applies to the default VRF. In cases where the preferred VLAN is not explicitly configured, the lowest VLAN with DHCP client enabled will be automatically selected. The no form of the command removes the preferred VLAN configuration and restores the default behavior. Public ip dhcp preferred-vlan 95 VLAN ID to set as preferred VLAN. VLAN identifier range: 1 to 4094. Specifies the VRF in which the preferred VLANshould be confi gured.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the dhcp-relay hop-count-increment command do?

The dhcp-relay hop-count-increment command enables the DHCP relay hop count increment feature, which causes the DHCP relay agent to increment the hop count in all relayed DHCP packets. Hop count is enabled by default. The no form of this command disables the hop count increment feature. Syntax: dhcp-relay hop-count-increment.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

The syntax of the dhcp-relay hop-count-increment command is: dhcp-relay hop-count-increment.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients command do?

The dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients command enables forwarding of packets from L2 VPN clients. Forwarding is enabled by default. Best practices is to disable this configuration on all the VXLAN tunnel endpoints (VTEPs), to avoid forwarding duplicate DHCP requests to the server. The no form of this command disables forwarding of packets from L2 VPN clients. Syntax: dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what is the syntax of the dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients command?
The syntax of the dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients command is: dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the dhcp-relay vsx active-active command do?

The dhcp-relay vsx active-active command enables DHCP relay active-active mode in VSX. When enabled, DHCP relay is active on both VSX Primary and Secondary peers. DHCP client requests are processed on the same VSX peer that receives them. A VSX peer that receives DHCP client request packets from its peer silently ignores them. By default, this feature is disabled, and DHCP request packets are forwarded to the configured helper addresses only by the VSX primary. The no form of this command disables DHCP relay active-active mode, and DHCP request packets are forwarded to configured helper addresses only by the VSX primary. Syntax: dhcp-relay vsx active-active.

The syntax of the dhcp-relay vsx active-active command is: dhcp-relay vsx active-active.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the dhcp-smart-relay command do?

The dhcp-smart-relay command enables DHCP Smart Relay on the device and on all the interfaces where IP helper addresses are configured. Disabled by default at the device level. Not supported on the management interface. The no form of this command disables DHCP Smart Relay. NOTE Prior to enabling DHCP Smart Relay, enable IP helper address configuration and configure secondary IP addresses on the interface. Syntax: dhcp-smart-relay.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the bootp <REMOTE-URL> command do?

The bootp <REMOTE-URL> command sets the BOOTP options that are returned by the DHCPv4 server for the current pool. BOOTP provides a way to distribute an IP address and boot image file to client stations. The DHCPv4 server returns the IP address and the location of the boot image file, which must be stored on an external TFTP server. The no form of this command disables support for BOOTP. <REMOTE-URL> tftp://{<IP> | <HOST>}/<FILE> Specifies the name and location of a BOOTP file on a TFTP server in the format: • <IP>: Specifies the IP address of the TFTP server hosti ng the file in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal nu mber from 0 to 255. You can remove leading zeros. For exa mple, the address 192.169.005.100 becomes 192.168.5.100. Syntax: bootp <REMOTE-URL>.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the default-router <IPv4-ADDR-LIST> command do?

The default-router <IPv4-ADDR-LIST> command defines up to four default routers for the current DHCPv4 server pool. The no form of this command removes the specified default routers from the pool. Specifies the IP addresses of the default routers in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. You can remove leading zeros. For example, the address 192.169.0 05.100 becomes 192.168.5.100. Separate addresses with a space. A maximum of four IP addresses can be defined. <IPv4-ADDR-LIST>. Syntax: default-router <IPv4-ADDR-LIST>.

The dhcp-server vrf <VRF-NAME> command configures the DHCPv4 server to support a VRF and changes to the config-dhcp-server context for that VRF. The no form of this command removes DHCPv4 server support on a VRF. Name of a VRF. Syntax: dhcp-server vrf <VRF-NAME>.

The disable command disables the DHCPv4 server on the current VRF. The DHCPv4 server is disabled by default when configured on a VRF. Syntax: disable.

The dns-server <IPv4-ADDR-LIST> command defines up to four DNS servers for the current DHCPv4 server pool. The no form of this command removes the specified DNS servers from the pool. Specifies the IP addresses of the DNS servers in IPv4 format (x. x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. Separate add resses with a space. <IPv4-ADDR-LIST>. The enable command enables the DHCPv4 server on the current VRF. The DHCPv4 server is disabled by default when configured on a VRF. Syntax: enable.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the lease {<TIME> | infinite} command do?

The lease {<TIME> | infinite} command sets the length of the DHCPv4 lease time for the current pool. The lease time determines how long an IP address is valid before a DHCPv4 client must request that it be renewed. The no form of this command returns the DHCPv4 lease time to its default value 1 hour. <TIME> infinite Sets the DHCPv4 lease time. Format: DD: HH: MM. Default: 01: 0 0: 00. Sets the DHCPv4 lease time to infinite. This means that addres ses do not need to be renewed. Public lease 171. Syntax: lease {<TIME> | infinite}.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the netbios-name-server <IPv4-ADDR-LIST> command do?

The netbios-name-server <IPv4-ADDR-LIST> command defines up to four NetBIOS WINS servers for the current DHCPv4 server pool. WINS is used by Microsoft DHCP clients to match host names with IP addresses. The no form of this command removes the specified WINS servers from the pool. Specifies the IP addresses of NetBIOS (WINS) servers in IPv4 fo rmat (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. Sep arate addresses with a space. A maximum of four IP addresses can be defined. <IPv4-ADDR-LIST>. Syntax: netbios-name-server <IPv4-ADDR-LIST>.

Specifies the NetBIOS node type: broadcast, hybrid, mixed, or peer-to-peer. <TYPE>. Syntax: netbios-node-type <TYPE>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the pool <POOL-NAME> command do?

The pool <POOL-NAME> command creates a DHCPv4 server pool for the current VRF and switches to the config-dhcp-server-pool context for it. Multiple pools, each with a distinct range, can be assigned to a VRF. A maximum of 64 pools (IPv4 and IPv6), 64 address ranges, and 8182 clients are supported on the switch across all VRFs. The no form of this command deletes the specified DHCPv4 server pool. Specifies the DHCPv4 pool name. A maximum of 64 pools (IP v 4 and IPv6) are supported across VRFs on the switch. Range: 1 to 32 printable ASCII characters. First character must be a letter or number. <POOL-NAME>. Syntax: pool <POOL-NAME>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the range <LOW-IPv4-ADDR> <HIGH-IPv4-ADDR> [prefix-len <MASK>] command do?

The range <LOW-IPv4-ADDR> <HIGH-IPv4-ADDR> [prefix-len <MASK>] command defines the range of IP addresses supported by the current DHCPv4 server pool. A maximum of 64 ranges are supported per switch across all VRFs. The no form of this command deletes the address range for the current pool. <LOW-IPv4-ADDR> <HIGH-IPv4-ADDR> prefix-len <MASK> Specifies the lowest IP address in the pool in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. Specifies the highest IP address in the pool in IPv4 format (x.x. x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. Specifies the number of bits in the address mask in CIDR format (x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 32. Syntax: range <LOW-IPv4-ADDR> <HIGH-IPv4-ADDR> [prefix-len <MASK>].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the static-bind {ip <IPv4-ADDR>}|{ mac <MAC-ADDR>} [hostname <HOST>] command do?

The static-bind {ip <IPv4-ADDR>}|{ mac <MAC-ADDR>} [hostname <HOST>] command creates a static binding that associates an IP address in the current pool with a specific MAC address. This causes the DHCPv4 server to only assign the specified IP address to a client station with the specified MAC address. The no form of this command removes the specified binding. Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255. The IP address must be within the address range defined for the current pool. Specifies a client station MAC address (xx: xx: xx: xx: xx: xx), where x is a hexadecimal number from 0 to F. Specifies the host name of the client station. Range: 1 to 255 pr intable ASCII characters <IPv4-ADDR> mac <MAC-ADDR> hostname <HOST>. Syntax: static-bind {ip <IPv4-ADDR>}|{ mac <MAC-ADDR>} [hostname <HOST>].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the clear dhcpv6-server leases command do?

The clear dhcpv6-server leases command clears DHCPv6 server lease information. The DHCPv6 server must be disabled before clearing lease information. Clears leases for all VRFs. <IPv6-ADDR> vrf <VRF-NAME> Clears the lease for a specific client on a specific VRF. Specify t he client address in IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: x xxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number from 0 to F. You can use two colons (: :) to represent consecutive zeros (but only once), remove leading zeros, and collapse a hextet of four zeros to a single 0. For example, this address 2222: 0000: 3 333: 0000: 0000: 0000: 4444: 0055 becomes 2222: 0: 3333: : 4444: 55. vrf <VRF-NAME> Clears leases for a specific VRF. Syntax: clear dhcpv6-server leases [all-vrfs | <IPv6-ADDR> vrf <VRF-NAME>] | vrf.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)
section: 4. Which VLAN is assigned a DHCP address?

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the guide say about 4. Which VLAN is assigned a DHCP address? ?

4. Which VLAN is assigned a DHCP address? : The factory default VLAN to which the DHCP address is assigned is VLAN1. Users can configure the VLAN for DHCP allocation on platforms supporting multiple VLAN configurations. Only a single VLAN can support DHCP assignments. 5. Can DHCP assignments be disabled in an HPE Aruba Networking AOS-CX switch? Use the no ip dhcp command on the required VLAN interface or management VRF port you can disable the DHCP assignments in an HPE Aruba Networking AOS-CX switch. A static configuration option is available on both ports. 6. Can IP DHCP switch assignment be carried out on a Routed only Port (RoP)? No, RoP is not supported. Only VLAN and OOBM ports can be allocated an address with DHCP. Public FAQ 204 7. Can an HPE Aruba Networking AOS-CX switch support DHCP IPv6 Allocation? Yes, an HPE Aruba Networking AOS-CX switch can support DHCP IPv6 allocation. However, for Zero Touch Provisioning (ZTP), IPv4 is required. 8.

topic: 4. Which VLAN is assigned a DHCP address?

section: Configuring DHCPv4 and v6 snooping over VXLAN over... 210

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, how do you configure DHCPv4 and v6 snooping over VXLAN over... 210?

To configure DHCPv4 and v6 snooping over VXLAN over... 210, follow the documented procedure: 1. Configure DHCPv4 and v6 snooping in the global and VLAN contexts with the commands dhcpv4-s 2. Configure the server connected port as trusted with the commands dhcpv4-snooping trust 3. Validate the DHCPv4 and v6 snooping configuration with the commands show dhcpv4-snoopin. Commands shown in the procedure include: vlan 110.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the dhcp-snooping authorized-server <IP-ADDR> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command do?

The dhcp-snooping authorized-server <IP-ADDR> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command adds an authorized (trusted) DHCP server to a list of authorized servers for use by DHCP snooping. This command can be issued multiple times, adding a maximum of 20 authorized servers per VRF. By default, with an empty list of authorized servers, all DHCP servers are considered to be trusted for DHCP snooping purposes. NOTE The mgmt VRF cannot be used with this command. The no form of this command deletes the specified DHCP server from the authorized list. Specifies the IP address of the trusted DHCP server. <IP-ADDR> vrf <VRF-NAME>. Syntax: dhcp-snooping authorized-server <IP-ADDR> [vrf <VRF-NAME>].

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the dhcp-snooping flash-storage [delay <DELAY>] command do?

The dhcp-snooping flash-storage [delay <DELAY>] command configures switch flash storage to be used for backing up client IP bindings (used by DHCP snooping). When flash storage is configured (and external storage is not already configured for this purpose), the switch stores the IP bindings in switch flash storage. When the switch restarts, it reads the IP bindings from the switch flash storage to populate its local cache. Writing the IP bindings to flash storage only occurs after the configured delay and if there has been a change in client IP bindings. Writing is skipped when client IP bindings have not changed since the previous write. Omitting delay <DELAY> sets the default delay of 900 seconds. Syntax: dhcp-snooping flash-storage [delay <DELAY>].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the dhcp-snooping trust command do?

The dhcp-snooping trust command enables DHCP snooping trust on the selected port. Only server packets received on trusted ports are forwarded. All the ports are untrusted by default. The no form of the command disables DHCP snooping trust on the selected port. Syntax: dhcp-snooping trust.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the dhcp-snooping verify mac command do?

The dhcp-snooping verify mac command this command enables verification of the hardware address field in DHCP client packets. When enabled, the DHCP client hardware address field and the source MAC address must be the same for packets received on untrusted ports or else the packet is dropped. This DHCP snooping MAC verification is enabled by default. The no form of the command disables DHCP snooping MAC verification. Syntax: dhcp-snooping verify mac.

The dhcpv6-snooping authorized-server <IPv6-ADDR> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command adds an authorized (trusted) DHCPv6 server to a list of authorized servers for use by DHCPv6 snooping. This command can be issued multiple times, adding a maximum of 20 authorized servers per VRF. NOTE Configure the link local IPv6 address instead of global IPv6 address of the DHCPv6 server as the authorized-server. For example: switch(config)# dhcpv6-snooping authorized-server fe80: : 2ca4: fa40: d4cd: bc2f The no form of this command deletes the specified DHCPv6 server from the authorized list. Specifies the IPv6 address of the trusted DHCPv6 server. <IPv6-ADDR> vrf <VRF-NAME>. Syntax: dhcpv6-snooping authorized-server <IPv6-ADDR> [vrf <VRF-NAME>].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the dhcpv6-snooping event-log client command do?

The dhcpv6-snooping event-log client command this command enables or disables DHCPv6 snooping client level event logs that help with client telemetry on a remote management station such as HPE Aruba Networking Central. By default, client level event logs are disabled. The no form of this command disables client-level event logs for DHCPv6 snooping after they are enabled. View these logged DHCPv6 snooping events by issuing the command show events -c dhcpv6-snooping. NOTE For additional information on DHCP-related event logging, please refer to the Event Log Message Reference Guide. Syntax: dhcpv6-snooping event-log client.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the dhcpv6-snooping tunnel vxlan trust command do?

The dhcpv6-snooping tunnel vxlan trust command enables DHCPv6-snooping trust on all VxLAN tunnels. The no form of the command to marks all VxLAN tunnels as untrusted. By default, all VxLAN tunnel interfaces are trusted. When trust is disabled on VxLAN tunnel interfaces: • DHCP broadcast packets are not forwarded on VxLAN tunnels. • DHCP server packets received on VxLAN tunnel interfaces are discarded. Syntax: dhcpv6-snooping tunnel vxlan trust.
